title: Russia's War on Ukraine – Topics - IEA
gist: IEA's overview of how Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine triggered the first truly global energy crisis, permanently reshaping oil and gas trade flows, collapsing EU dependence on Russian gas, and accelerating renewables to the point where fossil demand is projected to peak this decade.

insights.claim: Under today's policy settings, IEA projections show global demand for oil, gas and coal is set to peak within the decade, an outcome the agency frames as the biggest legacy of the energy crisis triggered by Russia's invasion.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A policy-driven peak in all three fossil fuels within ~10 years is a structural bear signal for long-dated fossil demand and capex assumptions.
insights.quote: demand for oil, gas and coal is set to peak within the decade
insights.locator: The biggest legacy of the global energy crisis

insights.claim: Government policy packages launched after February 2022 — including the US Inflation Reduction Act, Europe's REPowerEU and Japan's GX Green Transformation programme — are explicitly credited by the IEA with accelerating renewable deployment as an energy-security response.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Renewables tailwind is now anchored in energy-security mandates, not just climate policy, making it more durable across political cycles.
insights.locator: This historic shift comes as governments

themes.theme: Russian oil: volumes hold, revenues fall

insights.claim: Russia remained the world's biggest net oil exporter and third-largest producer (behind the US and Saudi Arabia) in 2023 despite sanctions, with export volumes stable year-over-year at 7.5 million barrels per day.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Sanctions rerouted but did not remove Russian barrels — physical supply stayed on the market, capping the price-spike thesis.
insights.quote: Russian oil export volumes remained stable year-over-year in 2023 at 7.5 million barrels per day
insights.locator: Russia remains a top oil exporter

insights.claim: Russian crude flows reoriented from West to East: exports to the EU, US, UK and OECD Asia fell 4.3 million b/d below their pre-war average while shipments rose sharply to India, China, Türkiye and the Middle East.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A durable redrawing of crude trade maps — India/China gain discounted barrels and bargaining leverage; tanker and insurance flows permanently shifted.
insights.quote: shipments rose sharply to India, China, Türkiye and countries in the Middle East
insights.locator: Russian oil export volumes remained stable

insights.claim: Russia's monthly average commercial-oil export revenue fell USD 4.2 billion year-over-year in 2023, driven by G7 price caps, widening discounts on Russian crude and lower global oil prices.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Price caps worked on revenue, not volume — the mechanism to watch for sanctions efficacy is the discount/cap spread, not export tonnage.
insights.quote: tumbled in 2023 by USD 4.2 billion year-over-year
insights.locator: Even so, the monthly average of Russia's export revenues

insights.claim: Russia's share of total EU gas demand collapsed from over 40% in the 2018-2021 average to roughly 10% in 2023, after Russia cut 80 bcm of pipeline supply and the EU absorbed 160 bcm of 'missing gas' in 2022.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: A ~75% structural demand loss for Gazprom in Europe is effectively permanent infrastructure realignment, not a cyclical dip.
insights.quote: In 2023, the share declined to roughly 10%
insights.locator: Natural gas from Russia met less than a quarter

insights.claim: Demand-side cuts — not supply substitution — did most of the work plugging the 2022 EU gas gap: industrial gas use fell ~25%, a mild winter cut consumption 10%, and renewables plus efficiency lowered demand another 10%, while coal filled under 4%.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The crisis demonstrated industrial demand destruction as the EU's primary balancing lever — a bearish read on European heavy-industry gas intensity going forward.
insights.quote: Gas use in industry fell by almost 25%
insights.locator: However, the main adjustments were on the demand side

insights.claim: US LNG met over 40% of the EU's 2022 supply deficit and the US reinforced its position as the EU's largest LNG supplier in 2023 as Russian pipeline deliveries dropped a further 38 bcm.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Structural demand pull for US LNG export capacity (Gulf Coast terminals) is locked in by Europe's pivot away from Russian pipe gas.
insights.quote: liquefied natural gas (LNG) from the United States – met over 40% of this deficit
insights.locator: According to IEA analysis, alternative sources

insights.claim: Global renewable power capacity is expected to reach 7,300 GW by 2028 under existing policies, with renewables overtaking coal to become the largest single source of global electricity generation by early 2025.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Renewables crossing coal as the top generation source is an inflection point for grid, storage and transmission demand.
insights.quote: renewables overtaking coal to become the largest source of global electricity generation by early 2025
insights.locator: Under existing policies and market conditions

insights.claim: Nearly 200 governments agreed at COP28 to triple renewable capacity by 2030 (above 11,000 GW), but the IEA assesses capacity is currently only on track for a 2.5x increase, leaving a deployment gap.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: The tripling pledge implies an under-build versus stated targets — upside for renewables supply chains if policy closes the gap, downside risk to climate-aligned scenarios.
insights.quote: with capacity currently on track to increase 2.5 times
insights.locator: At COP28 in Dubai, nearly 200 governments

themes.theme: Ukraine grid resilience and distributed energy

insights.claim: Russia has resumed systematic military targeting of Ukrainian power plants and energy infrastructure for a second winter, with improved air defences helping safeguard supply but structural power-sector problems persisting.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Recurring infrastructure attacks keep Ukraine reconstruction and grid-hardening demand elevated for equipment and financing.
insights.locator: In Ukraine, the energy sector is on the front line

insights.claim: The IEA argues distributed energy resources such as solar PV are strategically superior for wartime Ukraine because their shorter deployment lead-time meets shifting demand and reduces grid vulnerability to Russian attacks.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Conflict zones and fragile grids become a structural demand pocket for distributed solar and behind-the-meter resilience kit.
insights.quote: distributed resources – which have a shorter lead-time for deployment than conventional generation
insights.locator: These workshops had a strong focus

insights.claim: IEA members executed the two largest emergency oil stock releases in the agency's history in 2022 — 62.7 million barrels on 1 March and a further 120 million barrels on 1 April — the fourth and fifth collective actions since the IEA's 1974 founding.
insights.speaker: IEA
insights.so_what: Establishes the precedent and scale of coordinated drawdowns as a price-suppression tool to factor into oil-shock scenarios.
insights.quote: the largest stock release in the IEA's history
insights.locator: On 1 April, they agreed to make a further 120 million barrels
